case_name: Lake water pesticide and antibiotic control for groundwater recharge under high-recovery and no-concentrate preference
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for lake water containing pesticides, pesticide degradates, and an antibiotic for groundwater recharge while prioritizing high water recovery and avoiding difficult concentrate handling.

input_data.scenario_background: A regional water reuse project plans to divert seasonally impacted lake water to managed aquifer recharge basins during dry months. Monitoring shows a primary herbicide issue from simazine and its degradates, with lower-level terbufos and intermittent chlortetracycline associated with mixed agricultural runoff. The project owner wants a treatment train that reliably reduces trace organics before recharge, but strongly prefers near-complete water recovery and wants to avoid generating a membrane concentrate or other difficult liquid residual stream that would require off-site disposal.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Treated water should be suitable for groundwater recharge with robust reduction of trace organic contaminants and low risk of introducing persistent pesticides, degradates, or antibiotics to the aquifer.
secondary_goals: control particulate and organic fouling before recharge
provide stable performance under seasonal lake-water quality swings

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove suspended solids and reduce natural organic matter loading to downstream trace-organics treatment
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish particles and protect ozone-BAC performance
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize simazine-related organics and transform chlortetracycline while improving biodegradability of oxidation products
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: adsorb residual hydrophobic organics and biologically remove biodegradable ozonation byproducts
core_unit_functions.uv_disinfection: provide final microbial control prior to recharge

applicability_rationale: this route balances trace-organic control with the requirement for very high water recovery because it does not create a membrane brine stream
ozone plus BAC is well aligned with lake water containing moderate DOC where oxidation alone may form byproducts but BAC can polish transformation products
terbufos is hydrophobic and amenable to activated carbon capture, while simazine and some degradates benefit from the combined oxidation and adsorption-biological polishing approach
the train also improves particulate quality for recharge basin protection and is more robust to seasonal surface-water variability than adsorption alone
potential_risks: simazine degradate removal may be incomplete if ozone dose and BAC empty bed contact time are undersized
bromate formation risk must be managed during ozonation because bromide is present
BAC requires monitoring for biological stability and periodic carbon replacement or regeneration

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove bulk suspended matter and part of NOM
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: reduce particle carryover and stabilize downstream adsorber operation
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_contact: provide flexible seasonal adsorption capacity for episodic pesticide and antibiotic peaks
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: deliver longer-term polishing of pesticides and chlortetracycline
core_unit_functions.uv_disinfection: provide final microbial barrier before recharge

applicability_rationale: all-adsorptive treatment avoids liquid concentrate generation and supports essentially full water recovery
PAC plus GAC can be attractive where contaminant levels vary seasonally because PAC dose can be adjusted during runoff events
terbufos and chlortetracycline are generally favorable for carbon adsorption, and simazine can also be reduced although degradates may be less strongly adsorbed
this route is simpler than ozone-BAC from an oxidation byproduct perspective and can be easier to permit where bromate concerns are prominent
potential_risks: higher NOM in lake water can shorten carbon life and raise media replacement frequency
simazine degradates may break through earlier than parent compounds
PAC handling increases solids residuals and requires reliable dosing and sludge management

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce fouling load prior to membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: protect membranes by polishing residual particulates
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration: provide broad rejection of pesticides, degradates, and antibiotic compounds

applicability_rationale: nanofiltration is technically capable and offers broad-spectrum control for a mixed trace-organics problem including some smaller degradates that are less favorable for carbon adsorption
it can provide a strong compliance margin where recharge permits are conservative on trace organics
however, its ranking is reduced because the scenario strongly favors high water recovery and minimal concentrate handling burden
potential_risks: membrane concentrate management conflicts directly with the stated residuals constraint
achieving very high overall recovery can increase scaling and fouling risk
membrane integrity monitoring and pretreatment control add operational complexity

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove solids and reduce ozone demand from particulate matter
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize susceptible micropollutants and improve disinfection robustness
core_unit_functions.sand_filtration: remove oxidation-related particulates and provide physical polishing

applicability_rationale: this route preserves high water recovery and avoids concentrate handling
ozone can significantly transform chlortetracycline and contribute to parent pesticide reduction
it ranks below ozone-BAC because oxidation without a dedicated adsorptive or biological polishing step is less robust for simazine degradates and oxidation byproducts
potential_risks: ozone alone may not provide sufficient finished-water control of all degradates
bromate risk remains and must be actively controlled
without BAC or GAC, biodegradable byproducts and residual trace organics may persist

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove solids and reduce membrane fouling load
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: pretreat water prior to high-pressure membranes
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: provide very strong rejection of a wide range of dissolved organics
core_unit_functions.uv_advanced_oxidation: treat residual low-level organics in permeate if needed
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished-water chemistry for recharge compatibility

applicability_rationale: this route is technically the most aggressive barrier and can achieve very strong contaminant reduction
it is included because it is feasible from a removal standpoint for mixed micropollutant control
it ranks last because it is misaligned with the project's high-recovery and no-concentrate priorities and adds substantial cost and operational burden beyond what the case requires
potential_risks: RO concentrate disposal is a major unresolved burden for an inland recharge project
recovery losses directly conflict with the stated objective to maximize recharge volume
advanced oxidation after RO may be unnecessary over-treatment relative to contaminant levels

preferred_rank_pattern: high-recovery non-concentrating trains should rank above membrane concentrate-producing options
nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis may appear as technically strong alternatives but should not rank first under the stated constraints

must_reflect: importance of maximizing water sent to recharge rather than losing flow to reject streams
penalty for technologies that create difficult liquid concentrate disposal needs
need for a process train robust to lake-water turbidity and NOM fluctuations

must_contain: why adsorption and ozone-BAC are attractive for trace organics in lake water with moderate DOC
why simazine degradates are harder to control than some more hydrophobic parent compounds
why membrane options are downgraded despite strong contaminant removal capability

should_mention: carbon exhaustion or breakthrough under NOM competition
concentrate disposal burden for NF or RO
seasonal contaminant spikes from agricultural runoff
